notes: Official Origin product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Priya, I see you recently made a $1,650 payment to Bright Horizons Daycare on May 3 using your Chase Freedom Unlimited account. Finding a "smart" way to earn rewards on this significant recurring expense depends primarily on whether your center absorbs the credit card processing fees or passes them to you as a surcharge.
Since this involves tax strategies like FSAs and credits, I'll explain the concepts educationally. Per CFP Board standards, consult a qualified tax advisor for advice specific to your situation before making decisions.
Evaluating the reward vs. fee gap
Most daycare centers that accept credit cards through portals like Procare or Lillio face processing costs of 2.5% to 3%. If your center passes this fee to you, it almost always "wipes out" the value of your rewards. For your $1,650 payment:
At a 3% fee: You would pay an extra $49.50 in convenience fees.
At 1.5% rewards (Chase Freedom Unlimited): You would earn approximately $24.75 in points value.
Net result: You would lose $24.75 by using your card instead of a fee-free ACH transfer.
To make credit card rewards "smart," you first need to confirm if your specific Bright Horizons location absorbs these fees. If they do not, you should switch to ACH for the tuition itself and focus your rewards strategy elsewhere.
Higher-value strategic alternatives
Leverage a Dependent Care FSA
For someone with your income level, a Dependent Care Flexible Spending Account (DCFSA) is typically far more valuable than any credit card reward.
The math: You can contribute up to $5,000 pre-tax annually to a DCFSA. If you are in the 24% federal tax bracket and pay 5% state tax plus 7.65% FICA, using an FSA provides a "discount" of approximately 36.65% on that first $5,000 of care.
The savings: This saves you roughly $1,832.50 in taxes—far exceeding the $396 you would earn in 2% cashback on your estimated $19,800 annual daycare spend.
Target new sign-up bonuses
If you must pay daycare with a card and the fee is unavoidable, the only way to "beat" a 3% fee is by using the spend to hit a new credit card sign-up bonus (SUB).
Many premium cards require spending $4,000 to $6,000 within three months to earn 60,000 to 100,000 points.
Your monthly $1,650 daycare bill covers nearly half of most spending requirements on its own.
The value of a large sign-up bonus (often $600 to $1,000+) significantly outweighs a 3% convenience fee ($49.50 per month).
The Child and Dependent Care Credit
If you do not have access to an FSA through your employer, you may be eligible for the Child and Dependent Care Tax Credit. This allows you to claim a percentage of up to $3,000 in expenses for one child or $6,000 for two or more. While this doesn't provide "points," it reduces your tax bill directly. Note that you generally cannot "double-dip" the same expenses for both an FSA and this credit.
Would you like to see a comparison of how much you could save in taxes by maximizing a Dependent Care FSA versus your current credit card strategy?
